intitule: Lesson 25: Crime, Justice, and Social Issues - Vocabulary Recognition

enonce.texte: Exercise 1: Match the word to its definition. Draw a line or write the letter next to the number.

questions.texte: Match the following terms with their correct definitions.

sousQuestions.reponse: e. Criminal acts performed by minors (under 18 years old).
f. The act of taking someone else's property without permission.
a. Repeated aggressive behavior intended to hurt someone physically or mentally.
c. A financial penalty you must pay as a punishment.
b. A place where people are kept as a legal punishment.
d. Basic freedoms and protections that belong to every person.

intitule: Lesson 25: Crime and Justice - Fill in the Blanks

enonce.texte:
Exercise 2: Complete the sentences using the words from the box.
Words: court, offence, violence, punishment, forced marriage, crime rate

questions.texte: Fill in each blank with the correct word.
questions.reponse: 1. crime rate; 2. violence; 3. offence; 4. court; 5. punishment; 6. forced marriage

sousQuestions.texte: In Douala, the _______________ has increased because of unemployment.
The teacher spoke against _______________ at school after two students fought.
Stealing a phone is a criminal _______________.
The judge made a decision in the _______________ this morning.
For some crimes, the _______________ can be community service or a fine.
In some rural areas, _______________ still happens when parents force a child to marry.

intitule: Lesson 25: Identifying Social Issues in Cameroon

enonce.texte:
Exercise 3: Reading and analysis. Read the short text below and answer the questions.

In a neighborhood in Yaoundé, a 15-year-old boy named Paul was arrested for theft. He stole a phone from a vendor at the Mfoundi market. Paul said he dropped out of school because his parents could not pay the fees. He also admitted that older boys in his quarter forced him to steal, otherwise they would beat him. The judge decided to send Paul to a rehabilitation center instead of jail.

questions.texte: Identify the criminal offence mentioned in the text.
According to the text, what is one cause of Paul's juvenile delinquency?
questions.reponse: Dropping out of school because his parents could not pay the fees, and peer pressure/threats from older boys.

questions.texte: Justify why the judge chose a rehabilitation center over jail as a punishment.
questions.reponse: Because Paul is a minor (15 years old) and a victim of threats. Rehabilitation aims to correct behavior and reintegrate the child into society rather than exposing him to hardened criminals in jail. This respects human rights and focuses on education.

questions.texte: Recommend one solution to prevent juvenile delinquency in Cameroon based on Paul's situation.
questions.reponse: Possible recommendations: Free compulsory education to prevent dropouts; community programs to supervise at-risk youth; legal action against adults who exploit children for crime; awareness campaigns in neighborhoods about the consequences of theft.

intitule: Lesson 25: Dialogue Role-Play - A Bullying Situation

enonce.texte: Exercise 4: Complete the dialogue. A student (Maya) is talking to a school counsellor (Mr. Enow) about bullying. Use the vocabulary words: bullying, offence, witness, punishment, human rights.

questions.texte: Fill in the blanks to complete the conversation logically.
questions.reponse: 1. bullying; 2. witness; 3. offence; 4. human rights; 5. punishment

sousQuestions.texte: Maya: "Good morning, Mr. Enow. I need to report a case of _______________. Some students keep taking my lunch money."
Mr. Enow: "I see. Were there any _______________ who saw what happened?"
Maya: "Yes, my friend Linda. But she is afraid because they said it is a criminal _______________ to snitch."
Mr. Enow: "Don't worry. Bullying violates your _______________. Every student has the right to safety."
Maya: "What will happen to them?" Mr. Enow: "Their _______________ could be suspension and a meeting with their parents."

intitule: Lesson 25: Writing - A Letter to the Editor about Forced Marriage

enonce.texte:
Exercise 5: Writing Task. In the North Region of Cameroon, some young girls are victims of forced marriage. You are a student. Write a short letter (80-100 words) to a local newspaper. In your letter:

  • Define what forced marriage is.
  • State two negative consequences (for the girl and for society).
  • Suggest one punishment for parents who force their children to marry.
  • Use at least 3 vocabulary words from the lesson (crime, offence, human rights, court, fine, jail).

questions.texte: Write the letter to the editor.
questions.reponse:
Model answer: 

Dear Editor,

I am writing to denounce forced marriage, a serious crime in our country. Forced marriage is when parents marry off a child without her consent. This violates the child's human rights. The consequences are severe: the girl drops out of school and can suffer health problems. Also, society loses a future educated citizen. I recommend that any parent who commits this offence should face a heavy fine and up to 5 years in jail. The court must protect our children.

Sincerely,
A concerned student.
